section_path: AIM’s response to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: AIM®

section_path: AIM’s response to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Enforcement must come first: effective implementation of existing rules (DSA, DMA, CRD, UCPD, GPSR) before creating new obligations.

evidence_quotes.quote: or make platforms liable for third-country traders.

paragraphs.text: Require all traders targeting EU consumers to have a legal presence in the Union, or make platforms liable for third-country traders.

evidence_quotes.quote: Extend the “Know Your Business Customer” (KYBC) principle to all intermediaries, not only marketplaces, to ensure accountability and traceability.

paragraphs.text: Extend the “Know Your Business Customer” (KYBC) principle to all intermediaries, not only marketplaces, to ensure accountability and traceability.

evidence_quotes.quote: trusted flagger status under the DSA

paragraphs.text: Grant brands and right holders trusted flagger status under the DSA to act swiftly against counterfeit, non-compliant and harmful products.
Introduce effective “stay-down” obligations to prevent the reappearance of illegal or counterfeit content once removed.

evidence_quotes.quote: Address unauthorised sales explicitly at EU level by recognising them as unfair commercial practices.

paragraphs.text: Address unauthorised sales explicitly at EU level by recognising them as unfair commercial practices, ensuring consumer safety, brand integrity, and Single Market coherence.

evidence_quotes.quote: Establish a clear EU definition of “influencer” and proportionate liability across the value chain, with obligations applying to all influencers targeting European consumers.

paragraphs.text: Establish a clear EU definition of “influencer” and proportionate liability across the value chain, with obligations applying to all influencers targeting European consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative design and addictive features

paragraphs.text: Provide harmonised guidance and consistent enforcement of existing rules on manipulative design and addictive features. Do not introduce overlapping new laws.

evidence_quotes.quote: mandatory cancellation buttons.

paragraphs.text: Simplify consumer law by reducing duplicative information requirements, enabling digital-first solutions, and avoiding rigid “one-size-fits-all” obligations like mandatory cancellation buttons.

AIM – the European Brands Association represents over 2,500 manufacturers of branded consumer goods operating across the EU, both directly and indirectly through national brand manufacturer associations. Our members include SMEs and large multinationals producing food and beverages, personal, pet and home care products, and other household and consumer goods, as well as branded products in sectors such as apparel and toys. AIM’s members represent the branded goods industry, which drives innovation, investment, and consumer trust across the Single Market. These brands are central to daily life, built on trust, innovation, and responsibility.

The fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) industry is the third-largest manufacturing sector in Europe and a key driver of growth and employment. Every year, the industry moves €276 billion worth of consumer goods within the EU and exports an additional €174.8 billion outside the Union—demonstrating Europe’s global reach and the trust placed in European products.

The Commission has launched reflections on a Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Europe already has a dense consumer protection framework, but enforcement gaps, fragmented application, and loopholes exploited by rogue traders and counterfeiters persist. AIM’s position is that the DFA should not attempt to rewrite existing law.
AIM©

evidence_quotes.quote: creating an explicit EU-level prohibition on unauthorised sales
extending KYBC to all intermediaries
granting brands trusted flagger status to ensure rapid removal of counterfeit and unsafe products.

paragraphs.text: Instead, it should introduce targeted legislative additions where genuine gaps exist, alongside a renewed emphasis on consistent enforcement of the rules already in place. Key examples of such targeted measures include creating an explicit EU-level prohibition on unauthorised sales, extending KYBC to all intermediaries, and granting brands trusted flagger status to ensure rapid removal of counterfeit and unsafe products. In this way, the DFA can close loopholes without adding unnecessary complexity.

We agree with the need to address regulatory coherence and reduce unnecessary burdens, as highlighted in the Competitiveness Compass. The DFA should be developed with a clear understanding of its place within the broader digital policy acquis, including the DSA, DMA, GDPR, AI Act, and related frameworks. It should include a commitment to harmonisation across Member States and focus on enforcement rather than the creation of new rules. This will help ensure a coherent rulebook, reduce compliance costs, and support innovation and competitiveness in the Single Market.

section_path: 2. Enforcement of Existing Rules Before Creating New Rules

evidence_quotes.quote: Where this is not the case, liability must fall on the platforms that host them.

items.topic: EU Presence Requirement For Traders Targeting EU Consumers

evidence_quotes.quote: all traders targeting EU consumers should have a legal presence in the EU.

paragraphs.text: The rise of e-commerce has made it easier for **unsafe, counterfeit**, and other non-compliant products to reach EU consumers, particularly from outside the Union. Counterfeit goods are not simply an intellectual property issue — they are harmful products in themselves. They often bypass safety checks, contain substandard or dangerous materials, and can put consumers' health and safety at direct risk. In addition, counterfeit products frequently fail to comply with EU environmental rules, leading to the release of hazardous substances during production and disposal, particularly in high-risk sectors such as cosmetics. Consumers deserve the same protection online as offline. To guarantee this, all traders targeting EU consumers should have a legal presence in the EU. Where this is not the case, liability must fall on the platforms that host them.

section_path: 3. Fostering a Safe and Trusted Online Environment

evidence_quotes.quote: intermediaries must adopt effective "stay-down" measures.

This means that once a counterfeit listing or other illegal content has been removed, platforms must prevent the same product or materially identical content from being re-uploaded under a new account or slightly altered description.

paragraphs.text: The phenomenon of counterfeit products and so-called "dupes" promoted on social media is especially damaging, undermining consumer safety, eroding consumer trust, and devaluing legitimate brands. AIM believes individual right holders that satisfy the criteria should be granted trusted flagger status under the DSA to ensure swift takedown of counterfeit products – as foreseen in the DSA text. At the same time, intermediaries must adopt effective "stay-down" measures. This means that once a counterfeit listing or other illegal content has been removed, platforms must prevent the same product or materially identical content from being re-uploaded under a new account or slightly altered description. Without stay-down, rogue traders simply relist the same goods, creating an endless "whack-a-mole" effect that renders enforcement meaningless. Stay-down is therefore essential to ensure that takedowns provide lasting protection for consumers and effective deterrence against repeat offenders.

section_path: 3. Fostering a Safe and Trusted Online Environment

evidence_quotes.quote: **dark patterns**

paragraphs.text: Counterfeit sales are also often linked to **dark patterns** that deceive consumers. These include misleading product presentation (confusing layouts, deceptive images, or ambiguous product titles masking that an item is not genuine); hiding key information (origin, authenticity, or brand details buried in fine print or unnecessary clicks); fake reviews and social proof (manipulated ratings to make counterfeit items appear reputable); manipulative checkout flows (urgency claims such as "only 1 left!" or misleading discount pop-ups that pressure consumers into quick purchases); and difficulty in reporting or returning (deliberately complicated

section_path: 3. Fostering a Safe and Trusted Online Environment

evidence_quotes.quote: processes that obstruct consumer remedies once the product is revealed as fake

paragraphs.text: processes that obstruct consumer remedies once the product is revealed as fake). Such practices make it far harder for consumers to recognise counterfeits before purchase or to obtain redress afterwards.

section_path: 3. Fostering a Safe and Trusted Online Environment

evidence_quotes.quote: The KYBC principle, currently limited to marketplaces, should be extended to all intermediaries.

paragraphs.text: Accountability also requires systemic transparency. The KYBC principle, currently limited to marketplaces, should be extended to all intermediaries. Allowing anonymity enables rogue traders to operate with impunity. KYBC is a basic principle of business responsibility that should apply consistently in digital supply chains.

section_path: 3. Fostering a Safe and Trusted Online Environment

evidence_quotes.quote: carried out by operators without contractual authorisation

paragraphs.text: Although brands are increasingly relying on selective, exclusive, and direct distribution systems (exclusive and selective systems are recognised under EU law and case law as being pro-competitive),[4] [5] [6] there is currently no EU legislation in place to prevent unauthorised sales outside of these networks. Such sales of genuine products, carried out by operators without contractual authorisation, undermine the investments made by brands and authorised distributors. They also create unfair competition and deprive consumers of consistent product quality, warranties, and services. Although some Member States, such as France, have national measures in place,[7] cross-border unauthorised sales limit the effectiveness of enforcement and fragment the Single Market.

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

paragraphs.text: The issue of unauthorised sales should be tackled through the framework of the Digital Fairness Act and the wider Consumer Agenda 2025-2030:

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

paragraphs.text: Safety & recalls. Off-network sellers lack traceability into the manufacturer’s system, making recall notices impossible and safety instructions incomplete (wrong language, missing updates).[8]

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

evidence_quotes.quote: Consumers may unknowingly buy from a non-authorised seller and later find manufacturer warranties, repairs, spare parts or software updates are refused.

paragraphs.text: Warranties & remedies. Consumers may unknowingly buy from a non-authorised seller and later find manufacturer warranties, repairs, spare parts or software updates are refused.

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

evidence_quotes.quote: Authenticity & quality.

paragraphs.text: Authenticity & quality. Greater risk of altered, or expired goods, and of products not adapted to EU rules (labelling, instructions, conformity).

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

evidence_quotes.quote: Off-network sellers frequently imply they are “official/authorised” or that a “manufacturer guarantee applies” when it doesn’t—a classic misleading practice.

paragraphs.text: Transparency & deception. Off-network sellers frequently imply they are “official/authorised” or that a “manufacturer guarantee applies” when it doesn’t—a classic misleading practice.

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

paragraphs.text: Digital/cyber risks for connected products. Devices outside authorised channels may miss security patches or cloud services, undermining cyber-safety and data protection.[9]

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

evidence_quotes.quote: access to spare parts
frustrating right-to-repair goals

paragraphs.text: Sustainability & repairability. Unauthorised flows undercut repair networks, access to spare parts, and product passports/traceability, frustrating right-to-repair goals.

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

evidence_quotes.quote: Hidden costs.

paragraphs.text: Hidden costs. Apparent bargains can lead to higher lifecycle costs (repairs, returns, compatibility issues) and no local after-sales service.

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

items.topic: Harmonisation Of EU Consumer Protection Rules

evidence_quotes.quote: clear EU rules would level protection regardless of where they buy.

paragraphs.text: Single Market fragmentation. Consumers face uneven protection across Member States; clear EU rules would level protection regardless of where they buy.

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

evidence_quotes.quote: prohibiting unauthorised sales
off-network resale or misrepresenting their status
Unfair Commercial Practices Directive to prevent misleading practices.

paragraphs.text: AIM therefore calls for the introduction of EU legislation to adequately prohibit unauthorised sales, ensuring the well-functioning of the Single Market and a level playing field, both of which are essential to economic growth and EU competitiveness. This could be achieved prohibiting buyers from participating in off-network resale or misrepresenting their status, and by addressing unauthorised sales under the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive to prevent misleading practices. Strong enforcement tools at EU level would allow brand

section_path: 4. Addressing Unauthorised sales by an operator which is not authorised to sell branded products sold under tailored distribution networks (eg., selective or exclusive distribution, franchising, consumer ‘D2C’ sales)

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer marketing is an impactful form of consumer communication.
but it is also vulnerable to misuse.

paragraphs.text: Influencer marketing is an impactful form of consumer communication. It plays a legitimate role in brand engagement, but it is also vulnerable to misuse. AIM believes EU policy must distinguish clearly between two areas:

evidence_quotes.quote: Misleading and unsafe promotion

paragraphs.text: Misleading and unsafe promotion

evidence_quotes.quote: Some influencers promote unsafe, non-compliant or counterfeit products, misleading consumers and exposing them to risks.
AIM supports clear transparency rules, proportionate liability across the value chain, and a harmonised EU definition of “influencer” to reduce loopholes.
Obligations should also apply to influencers established outside the EU who target European consumers.

Trusted flagger status for brands, combined with the extension of KYBC obligations to all intermediaries, would be key enforcement tools to enable swift removal of counterfeit and unsafe product promotions and ensure accountability of rogue traders.

paragraphs.text: Some influencers promote unsafe, non-compliant or counterfeit products, misleading consumers and exposing them to risks. This includes cosmetics or toys without safety certification, counterfeit goods glamorised as “dupes,” or dietary supplements promoted with unsubstantiated health claims. AIM supports clear transparency rules, proportionate liability across the value chain, and a harmonised EU definition of “influencer” to reduce loopholes. Obligations should also apply to influencers established outside the EU who target European consumers. Trusted flagger status for brands, combined with the extension of KYBC obligations to all intermediaries, would be key enforcement tools to enable swift removal of counterfeit and unsafe product promotions and ensure accountability of rogue traders.
Responsible commercial communications

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencers are also widely used in mainstream brand marketing across many sectors, including food and beverages, alcohol, cosmetics, personal care and beyond.
In these areas, AIM members already apply strict standards to ensure marketing is transparent, responsible, and age-appropriate, with particular safeguards for children and other vulnerable groups.

paragraphs.text: Influencers are also widely used in mainstream brand marketing across many sectors, including food and beverages, alcohol, cosmetics, personal care and beyond. In these areas, AIM members already apply strict standards to ensure marketing is transparent, responsible, and age-appropriate, with particular safeguards for children and other vulnerable groups.

evidence_quotes.quote: transparency of commercial intent
avoiding direct targeting of children
ensuring age-appropriate influencers and audiences

paragraphs.text: These standards are well established through self- and co-regulation, coordinated at EU and global level by organisations such as WFA, the ICC and EASA, and enforced and monitored by national ad standards bodies. They cover issues such as transparency of commercial intent, avoiding direct targeting of children, ensuring age-appropriate influencers and audiences, and respecting existing EU rules on health and nutrition claims.

evidence_quotes.quote: responsible influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: AIM’s position is clear: the DFA should not attempt to legislate over responsible commercial communications. This area is already governed effectively by self- and co-regulation, which provides flexibility, rapid adaptability, and proven accountability mechanisms. The EU should continue to support this approach as the best model for ensuring responsible influencer marketing, particularly in sensitive categories.

evidence_quotes.quote: providing a universal definition of an influencer
ensuring disclosure of commercial intent
liability for unsafe or counterfeit

paragraphs.text: Influencers should not be stigmatised as a category. Many act responsibly, and brands have strong incentives to maintain consumer trust. EU action should focus only on closing genuine gaps: providing a universal definition of an influencer, ensuring disclosure of commercial intent, liability for unsafe or counterfeit
AIM©

evidence_quotes.quote: cooperation from influencers outside the EU targeting European audiences.

paragraphs.text: promotions and improving right holders ability to flag such content, as well as cooperation from influencers outside the EU targeting European audiences. Responsible influencer marketing in categories like food and alcohol is already well covered by self- and co-regulation, with mandates provided for in the EU's Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD), which the DFA should continue to endorse and support.

evidence_quotes.quote: brands are not held liable for the actions of influencers

paragraphs.text: Simultaneously, it is important that brands are not held liable for the actions of influencers, who have been recognised as independent traders. Moving towards such an approach would discourage brand partnerships and harm SME creators. Enforcement is more efficient when focused on influencers, the direct communicators with consumers, rather than multiplying complexity by involving brands. This proportional approach aligns with EU guidance and national precedents.

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns
unfair personalisation

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns, addictive design features, and unfair personalisation are often cited by the Commission as needing new intervention. AIM stresses that they are already covered under existing laws, including the UCPD, GDPR, and DSA. The real gap is consistent enforcement. The Commission should therefore focus on issuing harmonised guidance to clarify obligations, not on layering new rules that create duplication and legal uncertainty.

section_path: 6. Avoiding Duplication of Existing Rules to Protect Consumers from Unfair Personalisation, Manipulative and Addictive Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: On addictive features, greater consumer control is appropriate
safeguards should be in place for personalisation practices

paragraphs.text: On addictive features, greater consumer control is appropriate, especially for minors. However, prescriptive bans risk stifling innovation. Proportionate, risk-based measures provide the most effective balance. Similarly, safeguards should be in place for personalisation practices, but reversing the burden of proof or redefining the "average consumer" would destabilise legal certainty and increase litigation risks.

section_path: 6. Avoiding Duplication of Existing Rules to Protect Consumers from Unfair Personalisation, Manipulative and Addictive Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation is a cornerstone of modern ecommerce, delivering benefits to consumers by enabling tailored recommendations and relevant information.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation is a cornerstone of modern ecommerce, delivering benefits to consumers by enabling tailored recommendations and relevant information. The general use of personalisation should not, by itself, constitute an unfair practice and therefore it is essential that the DFA clearly delineates the scope of unfair personalisation practices. Accordingly we support a risk-based approach, where personalisation is only deemed unfair if it uses data in a manner contrary to established principles without the consumer's awareness on the processing and its potential adverse consequences. Clarity should be provided to distinguish legitimate personalisation from practices that may be manipulative and could bring harm to a consumer, ensuring continued consumer access to beneficial, relevant information without imposing undue restrictions on responsible businesses.

section_path: 6. Avoiding Duplication of Existing Rules to Protect Consumers from Unfair Personalisation, Manipulative and Addictive Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: Confusing subscription terms, automatic renewals and opaque cancellation procedures remain a frustration for consumers.
accessible cancellation options

paragraphs.text: Confusing subscription terms, automatic renewals and opaque cancellation procedures remain a frustration for consumers. AIM supports clearer transparency rules and accessible cancellation options, but opposes rigid obligations such as mandatory "cancel subscription" buttons, which create cybersecurity risks and reduce business model flexibility.

Simplification is equally important. AIM highlighted in its response to the Consumer Agenda 2025-2030 consultation that excessive, duplicative information requirements burden businesses and confuse consumers. Streamlining obligations, aligning timelines across legislation, and enabling more digital-first solutions would reduce costs and strengthen protection.
AIM

The Digital Fairness Fitness Check highlights challenges in the online environment, but AIM's analysis is clear: the EU already has extensive consumer protection rules. What is missing is robust enforcement and specific tools to tackle the gaps that undermine consumer safety and trust.

The Digital Fairness Act should therefore be conceived as a targeted legislative intervention – not a broad new regime. By addressing unauthorised sales through a clear EU-level prohibition, granting individual right holders that satisfy the criteria trusted flagger status under the DSA, extending KYBC obligations across all intermediaries, and requiring stay-down measures to prevent the reappearance of illegal content, the Commission can meaningfully strengthen consumer protection while maintaining a proportionate, innovation-friendly framework.

Targeted additions of this kind will enhance trust, level the playing field, and ensure Europe's responsible businesses and consumers alike benefit from a fairer and safer digital marketplace, whilst not compromising the EU's simplification and competitiveness agenda.

AIM (Association des Industries de Marque) is the European Brands Association, which represents manufacturers of branded consumer goods in Europe on key issues that affect their ability to design, distribute and market their brands.

AIM comprises 2500 businesses ranging from SMEs to multinationals, directly or indirectly through its corporate and national association members. Our members are united in their purpose to build strong, evocative brands, placing the consumer at the heart of everything they do.

AIM's mission is to create for brands an environment of fair and vigorous competition, fostering innovation and guaranteeing maximum value to consumers now and for generations to come. Building sustainable and trusted brands drives the investment, creativity and innovation needed to meet and exceed consumer expectations.
